questions.text: I document processes I've figured out so I or others can repeat them.
I experience loneliness as a quiet awareness rather than a sharp pang.
My emotional spectrum is real and present but it operates in pastel tones rather than neon.
I notice when a person offers unsolicited advice to subtly establish their expertise.
I feel that investing in my appearance, health, and skills is time well spent.
I keep track of projects mentally and trust I'll remember what needs attention.
I believe that life's best moments are the ones you didn't plan for.
I often present my thoughts as starting points for group discussion.
My emotional states color my entire sensory experience — food tastes different, colors look different, depending on how I feel.
When my expectations are violated, my system reacts as if to a physical shock.
I feel that earning more money for myself is one of the most practical goals I can pursue.
I believe that the moral priority of suffering is the principle I would invoke first when trying to explain my values to someone who does not share them.
I find that when I invest in myself, everything else in my life improves.
I find that when I have a choice between advancing myself and supporting someone else, I choose the latter.
I prefer clearly defined roles and expectations in collaborative work.
I instinctively take control of high-pressure situations.
When a conversation stalls, I push it forward with a concrete proposal.
I find that I am often unaware of the social cost of something I said until someone tells me later.
I hold the view that moral attention to suffering — the active decision to let it matter — is a habit that strengthens with practice.
I do not spend much time analyzing the subtext of conversations.
I believe that my ability to reason clearly about human problems depends on not being drawn into the emotional current of those problems.
I believe that approaching emotional situations with structured reasoning produces outcomes that are fairer to everyone involved.
I set weekly review sessions to assess what went well and what I need to adjust.
I believe that the capacity to process suffering intellectually, without emotional involvement, is what makes sustained professional service possible.
I sense the precise moment when a debate stops being about the topic and becomes about winning.
I reconnect with people when the impulse strikes rather than tracking our last contact.
I feel most like myself when I am part of an ensemble.
I frequently take on supporting or facilitating roles in team settings.
I do not usually detect when a social environment has an unspoken expectation about reciprocity.
I find that the emotional charge of a piece of music can override whatever I was feeling before.
I believe that treating others' suffering as morally binding is not a philosophical position but a lived practice that I try to maintain daily.
I believe my role in any group is to make sure everyone else has what they need.
I tend to consider how my choices affect the most vulnerable people around me before deciding.
I notice that dramatic revelations or plot twists — in life or in fiction — produce a mild stir rather than a jolt.
I often proactively define the criteria for who gets to participate in key discussions.
I intuitively understand the unspoken hierarchy in any social group I enter.
